article_title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed

content_preview.title: Astronomers Discover a Planetary System Whose Orbits Are Visibly Changing in Real Time, a Phenomenon Never Before Observed
content_preview.summary: The TOI-201 system hosts a super-Earth, a warm Jupiter, and a record-setting brown dwarf whose gravitational interplay drives orbital shifts observable on human timescales — within 200 years its planets will stop transiting.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Astronomers have uncovered a planetary system unlike any other in the known catalog: a three-body system 370 light-years from Earth whose orbits are shifting fast enough to track across a human lifetime. The discovery, published April 15 in *Science Advances*, is rare enough that researchers say it offers a live window into gravitational dynamics that typically play out over millions of years.

recommendations: 1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (956 words, within 400–1200 range). Clear section progression from discovery overview through system description, orbital dynamics, unresolved questions, and broader significance. Round 1 and Round 2 findings all addressed; Round 3 finding (Guillot/Triaud misattribution) corrected in this submission.
editor_notes.source_verification: Space.com: fetched and verified — star 1.3× Sun's mass and size ✓; TOI-201 d 6× Earth mass 5.8-day orbit (article says 5.85 days — minor rounding, non-material) ✓; TOI-201 b half Jupiter mass 53-day orbit ✓; TOI-201 c 16× Jupiter mass ~7.9-year orbit (article says 'approximately every eight years') ✓; 200-year transit cessation ✓; TESS and ASTEP instruments ✓. University of Birmingham: fetched and verified — star 30% more massive, 30% larger, about a tenth of Sun's age ✓; Gaia contribution ✓; ASTEP at Concordia Station ✓; Guillot quote: source confirms 'Tristan Guillot explained the system's significance: "this points to some active orbital reorganisation within the system, providing us a glimpse of what happens shortly after planet formation"' — correctly attributed to Guillot ✓ (Round 3 fix verified); Triaud 'peas in a pod' and gravitational interaction quote confirmed ✓. Phys.org: HTTP 403 to automated checker but manually accessible via WebFetch — Mireles quote 'This is one of only a handful of systems where planetary orbits can be watched actively changing on human timescales' confirmed verbatim ✓; second Mireles quote about characterizing the system's dynamics confirmed ✓; Dragomir quote about planet/brown dwarf boundary confirmed verbatim ✓; record claim 'longest-period transiting object ever discovered' confirmed ✓; Science Advances, April 15, 2026, Ismael Mireles lead author ✓.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All claims verified across three sources. The key Round 3 fix — changing 'in Triaud's words' to 'in the words of Tristan Guillot of Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur' — is correct per the Birmingham source. All prior round corrections remain in place: record claim properly attributed to Phys.org ✓, star age comparison attributed to Birmingham ✓, spurious 'in the act' quote removed in Round 2 ✓.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. No sensationalism. The 'peas in a pod' analogy is appropriately attributed to the scientific literature via Triaud's comment. The uncertainty about TOI-201 c's nature (planet vs. brown dwarf) is presented as an open question, not overstated.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Machine Herald article covers TOI-201 or this Science Advances publication. Distinct topic.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: All three rounds of review findings addressed. Sources verified independently. Ready for publication.
